event: The Expulsion of Adam and Eve from the Garden of Eden
symbols: angel driving them out — divine judgment executed upon human disobedience
nudity and shame — the loss of innocence after the Fall
Eve collapsed — the weight of sin and its consequences upon humanity
Adam shielding Eve — human solidarity in guilt and suffering
lush garden behind — the paradise lost, never to be reentered
dark clouds — the wrath and sorrow of God at the rupture of fellowship
description: A dramatic painting showing Adam and Eve being expelled from the Garden of Eden. An angel with dark wings and flowing drapery forcefully drives the pair out from the upper left, accompanied by smaller angelic figures or cherubim. Eve lies collapsed on the ground, her nude body turned away in shame, her long golden hair flowing. Adam bends over her protectively, his muscular body also nude, partially covered by leaves. The lush green vegetation of the garden surrounds them on the right, while dark, stormy energy fills the upper portion where the angel acts.
theological_description: The Expulsion from Eden is the catastrophic consequence of humanity's first disobedience — the moment when sin severed the intimate fellowship between God and His creatures. After Adam and Eve ate from the forbidden tree, 'the LORD God banished him from the Garden of Eden to work the ground from which he had been taken. After he drove the man out, he placed on the east side of the Garden of Eden cherubim and a flaming sword flashing back and forth to guard the way to the tree of life' (Genesis 3:23-24). The angel's forceful expulsion expresses the severity of divine justice — sin cannot coexist with holiness. Yet even in judgment, God showed mercy: He clothed their nakedness with garments of skin (Genesis 3:21), a foreshadowing of the sacrificial covering that would ultimately come through Christ. Eve's collapse under the weight of the curse — 'I will make your pains in childbearing very severe' (Genesis 3:16) — and Adam's protective posture reveal the altered nature of human relationships after the Fall. Paul interprets this event as the entry point of universal sin and death: 'sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people' (Romans 5:12). Yet he adds the redemptive counterpoint: 'For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous' (Romans 5:19).

scripture_refs.reason: God banishing Adam and Eve from Eden and placing cherubim to guard the way — the direct event
The curses upon Adam and Eve — the consequences of sin visible in their anguish
Sin entering the world through one man and death through sin — the theological interpretation of the Fall
Through one man's disobedience many were made sinners — the universal impact of Adam's fall
God clothing Adam and Eve — mercy amid judgment, foreshadowing sacrificial atonement

_meta.title: The Expulsion of Adam and Eve from the Garden of Paradise
